components-buttons-button--docs.title: Components/Buttons/Button
components-buttons-button--docs.content:
import { Meta, Title, Canvas, Controls } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks'
import { Design } from '@storybook/addon-designs/blocks'
import { A11yTestStatus, FigmaLink } from '../../storybook/components'
import * as ButtonStories from './Button.stories.tsx'

<Meta of={ButtonStories} />

<Title>Button</Title>

**Buttons** are used for actions, such as submitting forms or opening dialogs
and are typically placed in forms, modals, and toolbars.

<Canvas of={ButtonStories.Default} />
<Controls of={ButtonStories.Default} />

## Usage

### Kinds

<Canvas of={ButtonStories.Kinds} />

### Sizes

<Canvas of={ButtonStories.Sizes} />

### With icons

<Canvas of={ButtonStories.Icons} />

### With truncation

<Canvas of={ButtonStories.Truncation} />

## Accessibility

### Accessibility test status

#### General tests

| Test                                                                                            | WCAG Level                                                                                             | Status                                              |
| ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | --------------------------------------------------- |
| Button text conveys its meaning. The button's purpose is clear without relying solely on color. | **A** ([1.4.1 Use of color](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/use-of-color.html))            | <A11yTestStatus status="conditional" isManualTest/> |
| Primary variant meets color contrast requirements (4.5:1 minimum).                              | **AA** ([1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum)](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/contrast-minimum.html)) | <A11yTestStatus status="fail" />                    |
| Other variants (Plain, Critical) meet color contrast requirements (4.5:1 minimum).              | **AA** ([1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum)](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/contrast-minimum.html)) | <A11yTestStatus status="success" />                 |
| Buttons are at least 44 pixels wide.                                                            | **AAA** ([2.5.5 Target size](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/target-size.html))            | <A11yTestStatus status="success" />                 |

#### Keyboard navigation tests

| Test                                                                     | WCAG Level                                                                                             | Status                                  |
| ------------------------------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | --------------------------------------- |
| Buttons do not trap focus.                                               | **A** ([2.1.2 No keyboard trap](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/no-keyboard-trap.html))    | <A11yTestStatus status="success" />     |
| Focus indicators are clearly visible.                                    | **AA** ([2.4.7 Focus visible](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/focus-visible.html))         | <A11yTestStatus status="conditional" /> |
| The button focus indicator has conformant size, perimeter, and contrast. | **AAA** ([2.4.13 Focus appearance](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/focus-appearance.html)) | <A11yTestStatus status="success" />     |
| Buttons respond to keyboard activation (Enter and Space keys).           | **A** ([2.1.1 Keyboard](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/keyboard.html))                    | <A11yTestStatus status="success" />     |

#### Screen reader tests

| Test                                                                          | WCAG Level                                                                                           | Status                                              |
| ----------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------- |
| Screen reader announces the button role.                                      | **A** ([4.1.2 Name, role, value](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/name-role-value.html))  | <A11yTestStatus status="success" />                 |
| Buttons are announced by screen readers in the order they appear on the page. | **A** ([1.3.2 Meaningful sequence](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/meaningful-sequence)) | <A11yTestStatus status="conditional" isManualTest/> |
| Loading state buttons announce their state appropriately.                     | **A** ([4.1.2 Name, role, value](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/name-role-value.html))  | <A11yTestStatus status="success" />                 |

## API

### Props

In addition to the default `<Button>` component, there is also
`<PrimaryButton>`, `<PlainButton>`, and `<CriticalButton>`. Each of them has all
the props present on a
[HTMLButtonElement](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/HTMLButtonElement)
plus the following:

| Prop                | Type      | Required | Default | Description                                                                                     |
| ------------------- | --------- | -------- | ------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **`size`**          | `string`  | `false`  | `md`    | The size of the Button, which can be either `sm` (small), `md` (medium), or `lg` (large)        |
| **`isLoading`**     | `boolean` | `false`  | `false` | Whether the Button is in a "loading" or "busy" state while a command is executing.              |
| **`isFullWidth`**   | `boolean` | `false`  | `false` | Whether the Button should expand to the full width of its container element.                    |
| **`isMenuButton `** | `boolean` | `false`  | `false` | Whether the Button should be considered a `Menu` button. This will add a trailing chevron icon. |

Additionally, the `<CriticalButton>` also has:

| Prop          | Type      | Required | Default | Description                                                                   |
| ------------- | --------- | -------- | ------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **`isPlain`** | `boolean` | `false`  | `false` | Whether the Critical Button should have a more subtle, "plain" style applied. |

## Design

<FigmaLink href="https://www.figma.com/design/pDDlKkiRPt9NA2JdA6eyeY/%F0%9F%8C%AF-Burrito-Design-System-UI-Kit?m=auto&node-id=4456-16578&t=yqjWm5B67cALzovd-1">
  Button component in UI Kit
</FigmaLink>

### UX guidelines

<Design of={ButtonStories.Default} />

components-buttons-buttongroup--docs.content:
import { Meta, Title, Canvas, Controls } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks'
import { A11yTestStatus, FigmaLink } from '../../storybook/components'
import * as ButtonGroupStories from './ButtonGroup.stories.tsx'

<Meta of={ButtonGroupStories} />

<Title>ButtonGroup</Title>

A **ButtonGroup** is a horizontal bar of two or more visually connected buttons,
presented with no spacing between them. It helps users recognize related actions
and supports clean, consistent UI alignment.

<Canvas of={ButtonGroupStories.Default} />
<Controls of={ButtonGroupStories.Default} />

## Usage

### Default Usage

By default the `ButtonGroup` will group all children buttons horizontally and
adapt their border styles. On mobile devices, the buttons will stack vertically
to maintain readability.

<Canvas of={ButtonGroupStories.Default} />

### Using `IconButton`

The `ButtonGroup` component also works with [IconButton](?path=/docs/components-iconbutton--docs).
To follow the design spec make sure you add the `isAlwaysHorizontal` prop on the
`MenuGroup` (see ["Always Horizontal"](#always-horizontal) for more details)

<Canvas of={ButtonGroupStories.IconButtons} />

### Always Horizontal

To disable the vertical stacking on mobile devices, set the
`isAlwaysHorizontal` prop to `true`. To see how the example behaves on mobile
devices, see the [Always Horizontal](?path=/story/components-buttongroup--always-horizontal&globals=viewport:mobile1)
story.

<Canvas of={ButtonGroupStories.AlwaysHorizontal} />

## Accessibility

The ButtonGroup component is a visual wrapper that groups buttons together. Individual button accessibility is handled by the [Button](?path=/docs/components-button--docs) and [IconButton](?path=/docs/components-iconbutton--docs) components.

### Accessibility test status

#### General tests

| Test                                                         | WCAG Level                                                                                | Status                                              |
| ------------------------------------------------------------ | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------- |
| Button focus follows logical content order within the group. | **A** ([2.4.3 Focus order](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/focus-order.html)) | <A11yTestStatus status="conditional" isManualTest/> |

#### Screen reader tests

| Test                                                                   | WCAG Level                                                                                                | Status                                          |
| ---------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------- |
| Buttons are announced in the same order that they appear in the group. | **A** ([1.3.2 Meaningful sequence](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/meaningful-sequence.html)) | <A11yTestStatus status="success" isManualTest/> |

## API

### Props

The ButtonGroup component has all the props present on
a [HTMLDivElement](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/HTMLDivElement) plus the following:

| Prop                 | Type      | required | default | Description                                                                                 |
| -------------------- | --------- | -------- | ------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `isAlwaysHorizontal` | `boolean` | `no`     | `false` | Flag to indicate if the Button group should stack the buttons vertically on mobile devices. |

## Design

<FigmaLink href="https://www.figma.com/design/pDDlKkiRPt9NA2JdA6eyeY/%F0%9F%8C%AF-Burrito-Design-System-UI-Kit?node-id=18406-172579">
  Button Group and Split Button components in UI Kit
</FigmaLink>

components-buttons-unstyled-button--docs.content:
import { Meta, Title, Subtitle, Canvas, Controls } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks' import { Readme } from '../../storybook/components' import { Design } from '@storybook/addon-designs/blocks' import readme from './README.md?raw' import * as UnstyledButtonStories from './UnstyledButton.stories.tsx' Unstyled Button ## Usage **UnstyledButtons** are used for creating accessible buttons with custom styling needs.
📣 Note: In most cases, prefer using the standard Button component over UnstyledButton. UnstyledButton is designed for specific edge cases where you need full control over styling and the element is not a typical text-only button. It’s built as a `
` with role="button" and the necessary accessibility attributes, but should generally be avoided in favor of the standard Button for consistency and maintainability.

Only reach for UnstyledButton when you need to render something more complex than plain text and the standard Button does not meet your needs.

### Preserving Focus Ring Transitions Applying custom transitions to this component can unintentionally disable the transition effect on the focus ring. This is because consumer-defined transitions may override the component’s built-in focus transition. To preserve the intended focus behavior: - Use the [mixins.focus()](https://burrito-design-system.jane.qa/?path=/docs/foundations-mixins--docs#mixinsfocus) utility, or - Re-apply the focus transition manually. This is a subtle edge case that typically doesn’t require deep troubleshooting, but if you run into issues, feel free to ask for help in #ask-ff channel ## API ### Props Unstyled Button has all the props present on a [HTMLDivElement](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/HTMLDivElement) & `AriaButtonOptions<'div'>` plus the following: | Prop | Type | Required | Default | Description | | ----------------- | --------- | -------- | ------- |----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------| | **`disabled`** | `boolean` | `false` | `false`| If the button is disabled.|

components-form-inputs-checkbox--docs.title: Components/Form Inputs/Checkbox
components-form-inputs-checkbox--docs.content:
import { Meta, Title, Canvas, Controls, Subtitle } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks'
import { Design } from '@storybook/addon-designs/blocks'
import { FigmaLink } from '../../storybook/components'
import * as CheckboxStories from './Checkbox.stories.tsx'

<Meta of={CheckboxStories} />

<Title>Checkbox</Title>

Checkboxes are interactive form elements that allow users to select single
values or not. Additionally, in a group, users are able to select multiple
values as opposed to the Radio buttons where you can only select one.

See the related
[**Fieldset** component](?path=/docs/components-form-inputs-fieldset--docs)
for augmenting a set of Checkboxes with a group label (`<legend>`), helper text
and a validation error message.

<Canvas of={CheckboxStories.Default} />
<Controls of={CheckboxStories.Default} />

## Usage

### Basic

<Canvas of={CheckboxStories.Basic} sourceState="shown" source={{
  code: `
import React from 'react'
import { Checkbox } from '@janeapp/burrito-design-system'

export const MyComponent = () => {
  const [isChecked, setIsChecked] = React.useState(false)
  const handleOnChange = React.useCallback(
    (e: React.ChangeEvent<HTMLInputElement>) => {
      setIsChecked(e.target.checked)
    },
    [setIsChecked]
  )

return (

<Checkbox checked={isChecked} onChange={handleOnChange}>
  I have read and agree to the terms of service.
</Checkbox>
) }`}} />

### With label

<Canvas of={CheckboxStories.WithLabel} />

### Long Labels

<Canvas of={CheckboxStories.LongLabels} />

### Checked states

<Canvas of={CheckboxStories.CheckedStates} />

### Has error

<Canvas of={CheckboxStories.HasError} />

### Disabled

<Canvas of={CheckboxStories.Disabled} />

### Read only

<Canvas of={CheckboxStories.ReadOnly} />

## Accessibility

### Labels are required

All `<Checkbox />` components require a label for users that rely on a screen readers.

- If you don't want to show a visible label, provide an `aria-label` prop.
- If you want to show a label, provide one as a child of your `<Checkbox />`.
  Internally this is wrapped in a `label` element and will be linked to your
  checkbox accordingly.
- **Not recommended** If you would like to provide your own label outside of the
  `<Checkbox />` component, make sure the label has either a `htmlFor` or
  `aria-label` prop where the value is the id of the `<Checkbox />` you are
  labelling.

## API

### Props

The `<Checkbox />` component extends [`HTMLInputElement`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/HTMLInputElement) giving it access
to all the available attributes of a native `<input type="checkbox" />` element.

In addition to this, the following props are exposed:

| Prop                  | Type              | Required | Default     | Description                                                                                      |
| --------------------- | ----------------- | -------- | ----------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| **`children`**        | `React.ReactNode` | `false`  | `undefined` | The label of the checkbox.                                                                       |
| **`hasError`**        | `boolean`         | `false`  | `false`     | Whether the checkbox should display error styles.                                                |
| **`isIndeterminate`** | `boolean`         | `false`  | `false`     | Whether the checkbox is in an indeterminate state.                                               |
| **`readOnly`**        | `boolean`         | `false`  | `false`     | Whether the checkbox is in a read only state. If true, the checkbox is not editable by the user. |

## Design

  Checkbox component in UI Kit
</FigmaLink>

import { Meta, Title, Subtitle, Canvas, Controls } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks' import { Design } from '@storybook/addon-designs/blocks' import { Readme, FigmaLink } from '../../storybook/components' import readme from './README.md?raw' import * as TextAreaStories from './TextArea.stories.tsx' Text Area **Text Areas** are form fields that allow a user to write or edit multiple lines of free-form text. See the related [**Form Field** component](?path=/docs/components-form-inputs-formfield--docs) for augmenting the Text Area with a label, helper text and a validation error message.

README

EXAMPLES ### States Note that the placeholder text disappears when a `TextArea` is in a `readOnly` or `disabled` state. This is by design to prevent users from interpreting placeholders as actual values in those states where a placeholder is not actually helpful. ### Controlled ### Dimensions To specify the maximum width of a `TextArea`, you can apply the `max-width` CSS property. To specify the default height, you can apply the `rows` attribute (the default value is 3). The default minimum height is also the equivalent of 3 rows of text, but to override this you can apply the `min-height` CSS property. ## Design Text Area component in UI Kit

import { Meta, Title, Subtitle, Canvas, Controls } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks' import { Readme, A11yTestStatus, FigmaLink } from '../../storybook/components' import readme from './README.md?raw' import * as TextInputStories from './TextInput.stories.tsx' import * as NumberInputStories from '../NumberInput/NumberInput.stories.tsx' import * as SearchInputStories from '../SearchInput/SearchInput.stories.tsx' Text Input **Text Inputs** are form fields that allow a user to write or edit a single line of free-form text. See the related [**Form Field** component](?path=/docs/components-form-inputs-formfield--docs) for augmenting the Text Input with a label, helper text and a validation error message.

README

EXAMPLES ### States Note that the placeholder text disappears when a `TextInput` is in a `readOnly` or `disabled` state. This is by design to prevent users from interpreting placeholders as actual values in those states where a placeholder is not actually helpful. ### Types ### Controlled ### Max Width To specify the maximum width of a `TextInput`, you can apply the `max-width` CSS property. ### Number Input The `NumberInput` component is a thin wrapper for the `TextInput` with all the same props and behaviour except that the `type` attribute is not allowed and `inputmode` has a defualt value of `decimal`. To prevent the submission of non-numeric input, you will need to ensure that appropriate form validation is applied with this component. Read more about the [**Number Input** component](?path=/docs/components-form-inputs-numberinput--docs). ### Search Input The `SearchInput` component is a thin wrapper for the `TextInput` with all the same props and behaviour except that the `type` attribute is not allowed since it is set to `search`. Read more about the [**Search Input** component](?path=/docs/components-form-inputs-searchinput--docs). ### Date Picker While a Burrito `DatePicker` component is not yet available, it is recommended to simply apply the `type='date'` or `type='datetime-local'` attribute to a `TextInput` for accepting date input. ### Time Picker While a Burrito `TimePicker` component is not yet available, it is recommended to simply apply the `type='time'` attribute to a `TextInput` for accepting time input.

ACCESSIBILITY ## Accessibility tests status ### General tests | Test | WCAG Level | Status | | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------- | | Color is not the only method used to convey meaning
**Note**: When the Text Input is in an error state, it must be used with a `` for this criteria to be satified. | **A** ([1.4.1 Use of color](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/use-of-color.html)) | | ### Keyboard navigation tests | Test | WCAG Level | Status | | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- | | Text field is accesible via keyboard navigation. Using only a keyboard, you can navigate to the text field, type in it, and navigate away from it. | **A** ([2.1.1 Keyboard](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/keyboard.html)) | | | Focus indicators are clearly visible around text inputs | **AA** ([2.4.7 Focus Visible](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/focus-visible.html)) | | ### Screen reader tests | Test | WCAG Level | Status | | ------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------- | | Screen reader announces the purpose of the input is to type in text | **AA** ([1.3.5 Identify Input Purpose](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/identify-input-purpose.html)) | | ## Design Text Input component in UI Kit

components-typography-heading--docs.content:
import { Meta, Title, Canvas } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks'
import { FigmaLink } from '../../storybook/components'
import * as HeadingStories from './Heading.stories.tsx'

<Meta of={HeadingStories} />

<Title>Heading</Title>

**Headings** communicate the organization of the content on the page allowing
users to find the information they seek more easily.

<Canvas of={HeadingStories.Default} />

## Usage

### Basic

<Canvas of={HeadingStories.Default} sourceState="shown" source={{
  code: `
  import { Heading } from '@janeapp/burrito-design-system'

export const App = () => {
  return <Heading level={2}>Example heading</Heading>
}
  `
}}/>

### Levels

<Canvas of={HeadingStories.Levels} />

### Semantic Levels

To render a heading that will be presented to a web browser or assistive
technology (such as a screen reader) as a different level than its visual style,
simply apply the `aria-level` attribute.

<Canvas of={HeadingStories.SemanticLevels} />

## Accessibility

Applying headings properly is important to allow all users to quickly understand
the organizational structure and content of a page or view.

Users who rely on screen readers in particular are likely to
[use headings to find information](https://webaim.org/projects/screenreadersurvey10/#finding).

### Relevant WCAG success criteria

- [1.3.1 - Info and Relationships (A)](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/info-and-relationships)
- [2.4.6 - Headings and Labels (AA)](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/headings-and-labels.html)
- [2.4.10 - Section Headings (AAA)](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/section-headings.html)

## API

### Props

The Heading component has all the props present on
a [HTMLHeadingElement](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/HTMLHeadingElement) plus the following:

| Prop        | Type     | required | default | Description                                                  |
| ----------- | -------- | -------- | ------- | ------------------------------------------------------------ |
| **`level`** | `number` | no       | `1`     | Indicates which HTML tag to render and visual style to apply |

## Design

<FigmaLink href="https://www.figma.com/design/pDDlKkiRPt9NA2JdA6eyeY/%F0%9F%8C%AF-Burrito-Design-System-UI-Kit?node-id=4628-6703">
  Typography page in UI Kit
</FigmaLink>

> **Note:** In Figma, there is no **Heading** component, instead there are
> **heading** "Text styles" that are applied to text content.

<Title>Text</Title>

**Text** is a foundational component for applying typographic styles to written
content.

## Usage

### Basic

<Canvas of={TextStories.Basic} sourceState="shown" source={{

export const App = () => {
  return <Text>Some example content goes here.</Text>

<Canvas of={TextStories.Size} />

### Colors

<Canvas of={TextStories.Colors} />

### Custom tags (polymorphism)

<Canvas of={TextStories.CustomTags} />

### With Icons

Including a Burrito **Icon** component as a direct descentdent of the **Text**
component will apply alignment styles automatically.

<Canvas of={TextStories.WithIcons} />

## API

### Props

The Text component has all the props present on
a [HTMLElement](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/HTMLElement) plus the following:

| Prop              | Type                                                                                                   | required | default   | Description                             |

components-alert--docs.name: Docs
components-alert--docs.title: Components/Alert
components-alert--docs.content:
import { Meta, Title, Canvas, Controls } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks'
import { Design } from '@storybook/addon-designs/blocks'
import { A11yTestStatus, FigmaLink } from '../../storybook/components'
import * as AlertStories from './Alert.stories.tsx'

<Meta of={AlertStories} />

<Title>Alert</Title>

**Alerts** highlight important contextual information, feedback, or status updates
to make users aware of events or actions that require their attention. Elements
such as icons and colour, indicate the type and urgency of the Alert’s information.

<Canvas of={AlertStories.Default} />
<Controls of={AlertStories.Default} />

## Usage

### Default

The `<Alert>` component needs to be used with its subcomponents: `<Alert.Icon>`, `<Alert.Title>`,
`<Alert.Content>` and `<Alert.CloseButton>`. The `<Alert.Content>` is the only subcomponent required at
all time, you may use the other subcomponents as needed. For example, `<Alert.Title>`

```tsx
import { Alert } from '@janeapp/burrito-design-system'

export const App = () => {
  return (
    <Alert>
      <Alert.Title>Alert Title</Alert.Title>
      <Alert.Content>Use the information Alert to deliver neutral information.</Alert.Content>
    </Alert>
  )
}
```

### Tones

You can use the `tone` prop to change the tone of the Alert. The five tones are:
`success`, `critical`, `warning`, `info`, and `discovery`.

<Canvas of={AlertStories.Tones} />

### Closable

In certain cases, you may want to be able to close an Alert. You can do this by
adding the `<Alert.CloseButton>` component.

<Canvas of={AlertStories.Closable} />

### Different Icon and no icon

By default, the Alert will display an icon based on `tone`. You can use the
`hideIcon` prop to hide the default icon.

<Canvas of={AlertStories.WithoutIcon} />

You may also use `Alert.Icon` to render a different icon. Not that you will need
to pass the `aria-hidden` prop to the icon.

<Canvas of={AlertStories.WithDifferentIcon} />

### With Standalone Link

If your designs require you to have a full width link, you can use the `isStandalone`
prop of the [Link](?path=/docs/components-link--docs) component.

<Canvas of={AlertStories.WithStandaloneLink} />

## Accessibility

### Announcing dynamic Alerts

If your `Alert` gets triggered by a user change (as opposed as being present on
page load), you may want to make sure the content of your `Alert` is read by a
assistive technologies right away.

Use the [`aria-live`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/Accessibility/ARIA/Attributes/aria-live)
and [`aria-atomic`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/Accessibility/ARIA/Attributes/aria-atomic)
attributes to achieve this.

> **Choosing the aria-live value**
> As some assistive technology users can't "see" live updates, the `aria-live` attribute is used to define what updated information the user should be made aware of.

The `aria-live` attribute has three values: `assertive`, `off` (default) and
`polite`. Use `polite` in most situations as it's the least distruptive approach.
`assertive`should only be used when it's imperative that the user is made aware
of the change immediately.

```tsx
import { Alert } from '@janeapp/burrito-design-system'

export const MyComponent = ({ isAlertOpen }) => {
  return (
    <>
      {isAlertOpen && (
        <Alert aria-atomic="true" aria-live="polite">
          <Alert.Content>Use the information Alert to deliver neutral information.</Alert.Content>
        </Alert>
      )}
    </>
  )
}
```

### Setting a different level to Alert.Title

The `Alert.Title` component uses the [Heading](?path=/docs/components-typography-heading--docs)
component with `level={5}` under the hood. You can use its `aria-level` prop to make sure your page
follows a consistent heading structure

<Canvas of={AlertStories.DifferentHeadingLevel} />

### Accessibility tests status

### General tests

| Test                                    | WCAG Level                                                                                        | Status                             |
| --------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------- |
| Alerts meet color contrast requirements | **AA** ([1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum)](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/contrast-minimum)) | <A11yTestStatus status="success"/> |

### Screen reader test

| Test                                     | WCAG Level                                                                                                 | Status                                          |
| ---------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------- |
| Screen reader announces Alert content    | **A** ([1.3.1 Info and relationships](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/info-and-relationships)) | <A11yTestStatus status="success" isManualTest/> |
| Alert content is read in a logical order | **A** ([1.3.2 Meaningful sequence](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/meaningful-sequence))       | <A11yTestStatus status="success" isManualTest/> |

## API

### Props

The `<Alert>` component accepts all valid HTML attributes of an [HTMLDivElement](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/HTMLDivElement)
and a React `ref` will be passed onto the root `div` element that it renders.

In addition to this, the following props are exposed:

| Prop           | Type      | Required | Default | Description                                                                   |
| -------------- | --------- | -------- | ------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **`tone`**     | `string`  | No       | `info`  | The tone of the Alert. It will influence the component color and default icon |
| **`hideIcon`** | `boolean` | No       | `false` | Whether the default icon should be hidden                                     |

### Alert.Icon props

The `<Alert.Icon>` component itself does not define any unique props, but any valid HTML
attributes of an
[HTMLDivElement](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/HTMLDivElement)
or a React `ref` will be passed onto the root `div` element that it renders.

### Alert.Title props

The `<Alert.Title>` itself does not define any unique props, but any valid props of an
[Heading](?path=/docs/components-typography-heading--docs) component (minus the `level` prop)
or a React `ref` will be passed onto the element that it renders.

### Alert.Content props

The `<Alert.Content>` component itself does not define any unique props, but any valid HTML
attributes of an
[HTMLDivElement](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/HTMLDivElement)
or a React `ref` will be passed onto the root `div` element that it renders.

### Alert.CloseButton props

The `<Alert.CloseButton>` component itself does not define any unique props, but any valid HTML
attributes of an
[HTMLDivElement](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/HTMLButtonElement)
or a React `ref` will be passed onto the root `button` element that it renders. You will need to
use the `onClick` prop to close the modal.

## Design

<FigmaLink href="https://www.figma.com/design/pDDlKkiRPt9NA2JdA6eyeY/%F0%9F%8C%AF-Burrito-Design-System-UI-Kit?node-id=10393-9582">
  Alert component in UI Kit
</FigmaLink>

### UX guidelines

<Design of={AlertStories.Default} />

| Color alone is not used to convey meaning. The badge meaning is clear without relying solely on color. | **A** ([1.4.1 Use of color](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/use-of-color.html))             | <A11yTestStatus status="conditional" isManualTest/> |
| Badge content meets color contrast requirements (4.5:1 minimum).                                       | **AA** ([1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum)](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/contrast-minimum.html))  | <A11yTestStatus status="success" />                 |
| Badge meets color contrast requirements against the content background (3:1 minimum).                  | **AA** ([1.4.11 Non-text contrast](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/non-text-contrast.html)) | <A11yTestStatus status="conditional" isManualTest/> |

### Zoom magnification tests

| Test                                                                | WCAG Level                                                                                 | Status                                          |
| ------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | ----------------------------------------------- |
| Badges are visible and functional when screen is magnified to 200%. | **AA** ([1.4.4 Resize text](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/resize-text.html)) | <A11yTestStatus status="success" isManualTest/> |

### Screen reader tests

| Test                                                                   | WCAG Level                                                                                                      | Status                                              |
| ---------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------- |
| Screen reader announces badge content clearly and descriptively.       | **A** ([1.3.1 Info and Relationships](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/info-and-relationships.html)) | <A11yTestStatus status="success" isManualTest/>     |
| Badge icons have appropriate `aria-hidden` attributes when decorative. | **A** ([1.3.1 Info and Relationships](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/info-and-relationships.html)) | <A11yTestStatus status="conditional" isManualTest/> |

## API

### Props

The Badge component has all the props present on
a [HTMLSpanElement](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/HTMLSpanElement) plus the following:

| Prop | Type   | required | default   | Description                         |
| ---- | ------ | -------- | --------- | ----------------------------------- |
| tone | string | false    | `neutral` | Sets the colourway of the component |

components-card--docs.title: Components/Card
components-card--docs.content:
import { Meta, Title, Canvas, Controls } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks'
import { A11yTestStatus, FigmaLink } from '../../storybook/components'
import { sourceTransform } from './card.stories.utils.ts'
import * as CardStories from './Card.stories.tsx'

<Meta of={CardStories} />

<Title>Card</Title>

**Cards** are container components used to group related content, information,
or interactive elements in a visually distinct, modular way.

Panels are typically used to organize content within a user interface.

<Canvas of={CardStories.Default} source={{ transform: sourceTransform }} />
<Controls of={CardStories.Default} />

## Usage

### Default

The `Card` component needs to be used with at least one `Card.Content`
subcomponent.

<Canvas of={CardStories.Basic} sourceState="shown" source={{
  code: `
  import { Card } from '@janeapp/burrito-design-system'

export const MyComponent = () => {
  return (
    <Card>
      <Card.Content>Content block</Card.Content>
    </Card>
  )
}`}}  />

### Sizes

There are two different sizes of Card: small and medium. The default Card's
size is medium.

<Canvas of={CardStories.Sizes} />

### Tones

The `tone` prop controls the background colour of the Card. The default tone
is `default` (white). Use `neutral` for a subtle grey background.

<Canvas of={CardStories.Tones} />

### Responsive

A medium Card size will become small when it gets narrower than 320px.

<Canvas of={CardStories.Responsive} />

### Sample use cases

Some examples of how Cards can be composed with other Burrito components.

<Canvas of={CardStories.Demo} />

<Canvas of={CardStories.ClinicalDemo} />

## Accessibility

### Using Cards as landmark roles

Since the **Card component** serves as a container element, it is important for
developers to consider using it as a landmark for improved accessibility. By
applying an appropriate ARIA landmark role (e.g., `role="region"` or
`role="main"`), you can help assistive technologies identify the
card's purpose and structure within the page. Ensure the card has a meaningful
`aria-label` or `aria-labelledby` to provide context for users navigating via
landmarks.

**Resources**:

- [Using HTML landmark roles to improve accessibility](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/blog/aria-accessibility-html-landmark-roles/) on the MDN blog
- [Landmark Regions](https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/practices/landmark-regions/) - ARIA Authoring Practices Guide (APG)

### Accessibility tests status

### General tests

| Test                                   | WCAG Level                                                                                        | Status                             |
| -------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------- |
| Cards meet color contrast requirements | **AA** ([1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum)](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/contrast-minimum)) | <A11yTestStatus status="success"/> |

## API

### Props

The Card component has all the props present on
a [HTMLDivElement](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/HTMLDivElement) plus the following:

| Prop | Type     | required | default   | Description                                                      |
| ---- | -------- | -------- | --------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------- |
| size | `string` | No       | `md`      | Size of the Card component.                                      |
| tone | `string` | No       | `default` | Background colour tone of the Card. One of `default`, `neutral`. |

### Card.Content Props

The Card component has all the props present on
a [HTMLDivElement](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/HTMLDivElement) and no additional props.

## Design

<FigmaLink href="https://www.figma.com/design/pDDlKkiRPt9NA2JdA6eyeY/%F0%9F%8C%AF-Burrito-Design-System-UI-Kit?node-id=10971-9330">
  Card component in UI Kit
</FigmaLink>

components-datatable--docs.name: Docs
components-datatable--docs.title: Components/DataTable
components-datatable--docs.content:
import { Meta, Title, Canvas, Controls } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks'
import { A11yTestStatus, FigmaLink } from '../../storybook/components'
import * as DataTableStories from './DataTable.stories.tsx'

<Meta of={DataTableStories} />

<Title>DataTable</Title>

A DataTable is a container used to organize and display information in rows
(items) and columns (data point about the items). This allows users to quickly
scan, sort, compare, analyze, and take action on large amounts of data.

## Usage

### Default usage

The DataTable component needs to be used with all its sub-components. It's a
complex component and it is not as flexible as other Burrito components.
[Learn how it works under the hood](#how-it-works).

`DataTable.Table` only allows one `DataTable.Header` and `DataTable.Body`. The
number of `DataTable.Column` must match the number of `DataTable.Cell` (or
`number of cell + total colSpan`)

`DataTable.Row` must have a `key` prop containing uniquely identifiable data.

<Canvas
  of={DataTableStories.Default}
  sourceState="shown"
  source={{
    code: `
import { DataTable } from '@janeapp/burrito-design-system'
import { crocodileData } from './myData'

const MyApp = () => {
return (

<DataTable>
  <DataTable.Table aria-label="A list of crocodile facts">
    <DataTable.Header>
      <DataTable.Column>ID</DataTable.Column>
      <DataTable.Column>Species</DataTable.Column>
      <DataTable.Column align="right">Length</DataTable.Column>
      <DataTable.Column align="right">Weight</DataTable.Column>
    </DataTable.Header>
    <DataTable.Body>
      {crocodileData.map((croc) => (
        <DataTable.Row key={croc.id}>
          <DataTable.Cell>{croc.id}</DataTable.Cell>
          <DataTable.Cell>{croc.species}</DataTable.Cell>
          <DataTable.Cell>{croc.lengthCm}cm</DataTable.Cell>
          <DataTable.Cell>{croc.weightKg}kg</DataTable.Cell>
        </DataTable.Row>
      ))}
    </DataTable.Body>
  </DataTable.Table>
</DataTable>
) }`, }} />

### Column width

The `DataTable.Column` component offers three props you can use to set the
columns' width: `width`, `minWidth` and `maxWidth`. Three different units can
be used to set the width of a column: pixels, percentage and fractional
(`fr` only works on the `width` prop).

The width of a column will always be bound between the `minWidth` and `maxWidth`
if they are specified. `minWidth` defaults to 80 pixels, but we recommend setting
it to a value that will ensure the content remains legible.

#### Using percent and pixel units

The traditional pixel and percentage units can be used to set the width of each
column. The percentage unit will use the total width of the table.

You can leave the `width` of a column empty to designate it as the "fill" column.
It will automatically fill the remaining available space in the table.
If you need to have more than one fill columns, we recommend you use the
fractional unit (see below).

<Canvas
  of={DataTableStories.ColumnWidth}
  source={{
    code: `
  <DataTable>
  <DataTable.Table aria-label="A list of crocodile facts">
    <DataTable.Header>
      <DataTable.Column width="20%" minWidth={120}>Species</DataTable.Column>
      <DataTable.Column width={80} align="right">Length</DataTable.Column>
      <DataTable.Column width={80} align="right">Weight</DataTable.Column>
      <DataTable.Column minWidth={200}>Habitat</DataTable.Column>
    </DataTable.Header>
    //...
  </DataTable.Table>
</DataTable>
`,
  }}
/>

#### Using fractional unit

The [fractional unit](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/CSS_grid_layout/Basic_concepts_of_grid_layout#the_fr_unit)
is based on the unit used by the CSS grid layout. You can use it when you're not
prescriptive about the column width, but you need to set a weighted priority on
how each column will expand.

The fractional unit can only be used with the `width` prop of the
`DataTable.Column` component. We recommend you set a `minWidth` in pixels on
each column to ensure that content remains legible as the viewport gets resized.

<Canvas
  of={DataTableStories.FractionalUnit}
  source={{
    code: `
  <DataTable>
  <DataTable.Table aria-label="A list of crocodile facts">
    <DataTable.Header>
      <DataTable.Column width="1fr">ID</DataTable.Column>
      <DataTable.Column width="2fr" minWidth={120}>Species</DataTable.Column>
      <DataTable.Column width="1fr" align="right">Length</DataTable.Column>
      <DataTable.Column width="1fr" align="right">Weight</DataTable.Column>
      <DataTable.Column width="5fr" minWidth={200}>Habitat</DataTable.Column>
    </DataTable.Header>
    //...
  </DataTable.Table>
</DataTable>
`,
  }}
/>

### Hugging content

Although the recommended width for a table is to fill its container
(`width: 100%`) it is also possible to make the table hug its content and take
the least amount of space.

<Canvas
  of={DataTableStories.HuggingContent}
  source={{
    code: `
  <DataTable style={{ width: 'max-content' }}>
  <DataTable.Table style={{ width: 'auto' }}>
    //...
  </DataTable.Table>
</DataTable>
`,
  }}
/>

### Content alignment

You can use the `align` prop on the `DataTable.Column` and `DataTable.Cell`
components to set the text alignment of their content. This prop can take the
values `left` and `right` and default to `left`. The `DataTable.Cell` component
will inherit the `align` value from its corresponding column but you can
override the value for a specific cell if required.

<Canvas of={DataTableStories.ContentAlignment} />

### Buttons and Badges

Actions like buttons and overflow menus can simply be included in a table,
typically at the end of a row. If the column does not have a visible header
cell, then a "visually hidden" label should be included in its place or
accessiblity. You can use the
[**VisuallyHidden**](https://react-spectrum.adobe.com/react-aria/VisuallyHidden.html)
component from `react-aria` for this.

<Canvas
  of={DataTableStories.ButtonsAndBadges}
  source={{
    code: `
<DataTable>
  <DataTable.Table aria-label="A list of crocodile facts">
    <DataTable.Header>
      <DataTable.Column>ID</DataTable.Column>     
      <DataTable.Column width="100fr">Species</DataTable.Column>
      <DataTable.Column align="right">Length</DataTable.Column>
      <DataTable.Column align="right">Weight</DataTable.Column>
      <DataTable.Column>Diet</DataTable.Column>
      <DataTable.Column align="right">
        <VisuallyHidden>edit record</VisuallyHidden>
      </DataTable.Column>
      <DataTable.Column minWidth={64}>
        <VisuallyHidden>actions</VisuallyHidden>
      </DataTable.Column>
    </DataTable.Header>
    <DataTable.Body>  
      {crocodileData.map((croc) => (
        <DataTable.Row key={croc.id}>
          <DataTable.Cell>{croc.id}</DataTable.Cell>
          <DataTable.Cell>{croc.species}</DataTable.Cell>
          <DataTable.Cell>{croc.lengthCm}cm</DataTable.Cell>
          <DataTable.Cell>{croc.weightKg}kg</DataTable.Cell>
          <DataTable.Cell>
            <Badge tone="warning">Meat</Badge>
          </DataTable.Cell>
          <DataTable.Cell>
            <Button>Edit</Button>
          </DataTable.Cell>
          <DataTable.Cell>
            <IconBasicButton aria-label="overflow menu">
              <Ellipsis aria-hidden />
            </IconBasicButton>
          </DataTable.Cell>
        </DataTable.Row>
      ))}
    </DataTable.Body>
  </DataTable.Table>
</DataTable>
`,
  }}
/>{' '}

### Cells spanning multiple columns

The `DataTable.Cell` component supports the `colSpan` prop, which allows a cell
to span multiple columns.

<Canvas
  of={DataTableStories.ColSpan}
  source={{
    code: `
<DataTable>
  <DataTable.Table aria-label="A list of crocodile facts">
    <DataTable.Header>
      <DataTable.Column>Species</DataTable.Column>
      <DataTable.Column align="right">Length</DataTable.Column>
      <DataTable.Column align="right">Weight</DataTable.Column>
    </DataTable.Header>
    <DataTable.Body>
      <>
        {crocodileData.map((croc) => (
          <DataTable.Row key={croc.id}>
            <DataTable.Cell>{croc.species}</DataTable.Cell>
            <DataTable.Cell>{croc.lengthCm}cm</DataTable.Cell>
            <DataTable.Cell>{croc.weightKg}kg</DataTable.Cell>
          </DataTable.Row>
        ))}
        <DataTable.Row>
          <DataTable.Cell colSpan={3}>
            <Text style={{ textAlign: 'center' }}>
              This is a row with a cell that spans all columns. It can be used for summaries or additional
              information.
            </Text>
          </DataTable.Cell>
        </DataTable.Row>
      </>
    </DataTable.Body>
  </DataTable.Table>
</DataTable>
`,
  }}
/>

### Scrolling

#### Vertical scrolling

By default, the DataTable will expand vertically to match the number of rows
present in the Table. You can limit that length using CSS and the DataTable
will become scrollable.

<Canvas of={DataTableStories.VerticalScrolling} />

#### Horizontal scrolling

The horizontal scroll will be toggled when the width of the DataTable is less
than the sum of all the columns' `width` and `minWidth`.

A shadow will appear on either side when more scrollable content is present.

<Canvas of={DataTableStories.HorizontalScrolling} />

### States

#### Loading state

The `DataTable.LoadingState` component can be used to display a loading state
while the data is being fetched. It will render a series of rows and columns
with `SkeletonBar` components. It must be included as an immediate descendent of
`DataTable.Body`. The number of rows can be set using the `rowCount` prop
(defaults to 3).

<Canvas
  of={DataTableStories.LoadingState}
  source={{
    code: `
<DataTable>
  <DataTable.Table aria-label="A list of crocodile facts">
    <DataTable.Header>
      <DataTable.Column>ID</DataTable.Column>
      <DataTable.Column>Species</DataTable.Column>
      <DataTable.Column align="right">Length</DataTable.Column>
      <DataTable.Column align="right">Weight</DataTable.Column>
    </DataTable.Header>
    <DataTable.Body>
      <DataTable.LoadingState rowCount={5} />
    </DataTable.Body>
  </DataTable.Table>
</DataTable>
`,
  }}
/>

#### Empty state

The `DataTable.EmptyState` component can be used to display an empty state when
there is no data to display. It must be included as an immediate descendent of
`DataTable.Body`. It will render an illustration with a generic message that can
be overridden by providing `children`.

<Canvas
  of={DataTableStories.EmptyStateDefault}
  source={{
    code: `
<DataTable>
  <DataTable.Table aria-label="A list of crocodile facts">
    <DataTable.Header>
      <DataTable.Column>ID</DataTable.Column>
      <DataTable.Column>Species</DataTable.Column>
      <DataTable.Column align="right">Length</DataTable.Column>
      <DataTable.Column align="right">Weight</DataTable.Column>
    </DataTable.Header>     
    <DataTable.Body>
      <DataTable.EmptyState />
    </DataTable.Body>
  </DataTable.Table>
</DataTable>
`,
  }}
/>

#### Empty state with custom content

<Canvas
  of={DataTableStories.EmptyStateCustom}
  source={{
    code: `
<DataTable>
  <DataTable.Table aria-label="A list of crocodile facts">
    <DataTable.Header>
      <DataTable.Column>ID</DataTable.Column>
      <DataTable.Column>Species</DataTable.Column>
      <DataTable.Column align="right">Length</DataTable.Column>
      <DataTable.Column align="right">Weight</DataTable.Column>
    </DataTable.Header>     
    <DataTable.Body>
      <DataTable.EmptyState>
        <Heading level={4}>No results found</Heading>
        <Text style={{ marginTop: vars.global.size['1x'] }}>Try adjusting your filters or add new data.</Text>
        <Text>
          <Button>Take action</Button>
        </Text>
        <Text>
          <Link href="">Learn more about some topic</Link>
        </Text>
      </DataTable.EmptyState>
    </DataTable.Body>
  </DataTable.Table>
</DataTable>
`,
  }}
/>

#### Error state

The `DataTable.ErrorState` component can be used to display an error state when
there is an error fetching the data. It must be included as an immediate
descendent of `DataTable.Body`. It will render an illustration with a generic
message that can be overridden by providing `children`.

<Canvas
  of={DataTableStories.ErrorStateDefault}
  source={{
    code: `
<DataTable>
  <DataTable.Table aria-label="A list of crocodile facts">
    <DataTable.Header>
      <DataTable.Column>ID</DataTable.Column>
      <DataTable.Column>Species</DataTable.Column>
      <DataTable.Column align="right">Length</DataTable.Column>   
      <DataTable.Column align="right">Weight</DataTable.Column>
    </DataTable.Header>
    <DataTable.Body>
      <DataTable.ErrorState />
    </DataTable.Body>
  </DataTable.Table>
</DataTable>
`,
  }}
/>

#### Error state with custom content

<Canvas
  of={DataTableStories.ErrorStateCustom}
  source={{
    code: `
<DataTable>
  <DataTable.Table aria-label="A list of crocodile facts">
    <DataTable.Header>
      <DataTable.Column>ID</DataTable.Column>
      <DataTable.Column>Species</DataTable.Column>
      <DataTable.Column align="right">Length</DataTable.Column>   
      <DataTable.Column align="right">Weight</DataTable.Column>
    </DataTable.Header>
    <DataTable.Body>
      <DataTable.ErrorState>
        <Heading level={4}>Something went wrong</Heading>
        <Text style={{ marginTop: vars.global.size['1x'] }}>We couldn't load your data. Please try again.</Text>
        <Text>
          <Button>Retry</Button>
        </Text>
        <Text>
          <Link>Learn more about some topic</Link>
        </Text>
      </DataTable.ErrorState>
    </DataTable.Body>
  </DataTable.Table>
</DataTable>
`,
  }}
/>

### Sorting

The `DataTable` component supports sorting by clicking the column header.

To enable sorting for a column, you must include the `allowsSorting` prop on the
corresponding `DataTable.Column`, along with a `key` prop that uniquely
identifies the column.

To apply the desired sort to your data, you will need to provide a callback
function to the `onSort` prop for the root `DataTable` component, as well as an
object that describes the `column` and `direction` of the sort to the
`appliedSort` prop. Refer to the [props table](#props) below for more details.

<Canvas
  of={DataTableStories.Sorting}
  source={{
    code: `
<DataTable appliedSort={appliedSort} onSort={(appliedSort) => { ... }}>
  <DataTable.Table aria-label="A list of crocodile facts">
    <DataTable.Header>
      <DataTable.Column allowsSorting key="id">ID</DataTable.Column>
      <DataTable.Column allowsSorting key="species">Species</DataTable.Column>        
      <DataTable.Column allowsSorting key="length" align="right">Length</DataTable.Column>
      <DataTable.Column allowsSorting key="weight" align="right">Weight</DataTable.Column>
    </DataTable.Header>
    <DataTable.Body>
      {crocodileData.map((croc) => (
        <DataTable.Row key={croc.id}>   
          <DataTable.Cell>{croc.id}</DataTable.Cell>
          <DataTable.Cell>{croc.species}</DataTable.Cell>
          <DataTable.Cell>{croc.lengthCm}cm</DataTable.Cell>
          <DataTable.Cell>{croc.weightKg}kg</DataTable.Cell>
        </DataTable.Row>
      ))}
    </DataTable.Body>
  </DataTable.Table>
</DataTable>
`,
  }}
/>

See the full source code for this example in
[DataTable.stories.tsx](https://github.com/janeapp/burrito-design-system/blob/main/src/components/DataTable/DataTable.stories.tsx).

#### Asynchronous sorting

In many cases, applying a sort will require fetching new data from an endpoint
asynchronously. In this case, you can use the `onSort` callback to trigger a
data fetch and update the `appliedSort` prop with the new sort once the data is
fetched.

For an example, see the
[Async Sorting](?path=/story/components-datatable--async-loading) story and
corresponding source code in
[DataTable.stories.tsx](https://github.com/janeapp/burrito-design-system/blob/main/src/components/DataTable/DataTable.stories.tsx).

### Dynamic rendering

An alternative pattern for rendering a `DataTable` is to provide the column
schema and data rows to the `DataTable.Header` and `DataTable.Body`
respectively, and utilize a render function for `DataTable.Columns`,
`DataTable.Rows`, and `DataTable.Cells`.

For more information, see the
[React Aria Table documentation](https://react-spectrum.adobe.com/react-aria/Table.html#content).

<Canvas
  of={DataTableStories.RenderProps}
  source={{
    code: `
const columns = [
  { key: 'id', label: 'ID', isRowHeader: true },
  { key: 'species', label: 'Species' },
  { key: 'lengthCm', label: 'Length', align: 'right' },
  { key: 'weightKg', label: 'Weight', align: 'right' },
]
return (
  <DataTable>
    <DataTable.Table aria-label="A list of crocodile facts">
      <DataTable.Header columns={columns}>
        {(column) => (
          <DataTable.Column isRowHeader={column.isRowHeader} align={column.align}>
            {column.label}
          </DataTable.Column>
        )}
      </DataTable.Header>
      <DataTable.Body items={crocodileData}>
        {(item) => (
          <DataTable.Row>
            {(columnKey) => (
              <DataTable.Cell>{item[columnKey]}</DataTable.Cell>
            )}
          </DataTable.Row>
        )}
      </DataTable.Body>
    </DataTable.Table>
  </DataTable>
)
`,
  }}
/>

### Using an error boundary

Because the `DataTable` component expects a specific JSX structure which may be
composed dynamically, it is recommended to wrap it in a React `ErrorBoundary`
component to prevent crashing the entire application if the JSX is invalid.

For a more detailed explanation,
[learn how the DataTable works under the hood](#how-it-works).

<Canvas
  of={DataTableStories.ErrorBoundary}
  source={{
    code: `
const fallback = (
  <>
    <Heading level={4}>Something went wrong</Heading>
    <Text>We couldn't load your data. </Text>
  </>
)
return (
  <ErrorBoundary fallback={fallback}>
    <DataTable>
      <DataTable.Table aria-label="A list of crocodile facts"></DataTable.Table>
    </DataTable>
  </ErrorBoundary>
)
`,
  }}
/>

## Accessibility

### Identify header cells for a row

Use `isRowHeader` on the column that contains uniquely identifiable data.

As seen in [`DataTable.Column` props](#datatablecolumn-props) the `isRowHeader`
prop is used to establish a relationship between with the entire row. By default
`isRowHeader` will be assigned to the first column of your table. But you should
ensure that it is assigned to the column that contains the most uniquely
identifiable data.

<Canvas
  of={DataTableStories.RowHeader}
  source={{
    code: `
  <DataTable>
    <DataTable.Table aria-label="A list of crocodile facts">
      <DataTable.Header>
        <DataTable.Column minWidth={120} width="3fr">
          Species
        </DataTable.Column>
        <DataTable.Column minWidth={80} width="1fr" isRowHeader>
          ID
        </DataTable.Column>
        <DataTable.Column width="1fr" align="right">
          Length
        </DataTable.Column>
        <DataTable.Column width="1fr" align="right">
          Weight
        </DataTable.Column>
      </DataTable.Header>
      <DataTable.Body>
        {crocodileData.map((croc) => (
          // ...
        ))}
      </DataTable.Body>
    </DataTable.Table>
  </DataTable>
  `,
  }}
/>

### Right align numerical data

To simplify readability make sure to right align the text on numerical column
(as well as the header)

<Canvas
  of={DataTableStories.Default}
  source={{
    code: `
  <DataTable>
    <DataTable.Table aria-label="A list of crocodile facts">
      <DataTable.Header>
        <DataTable.Column align="right">Length</DataTable.Column>
      </DataTable.Header>
      <DataTable.Body>
        {crocodileData.map((croc) => (
          <DataTable.Row key={croc.id}>
            <DataTable.Cell>{croc.lengthCm}cm</DataTable.Cell>
          </DataTable.Row>
        ))}
      </DataTable.Body>
    </DataTable.Table>
  </DataTable>
`,
  }}
/>

### Label the table with `label` or `labelled-by`

To ensure that users using screen reader can understand what the table
represents, make sure to use `aria-label` or `aria-labelledby` to give
additional context.

#### Using `aria-label`

<Canvas
  of={DataTableStories.Default}
  source={{
    code: `
    <DataTable.Table aria-label="A list of crocodile facts" />
`,
  }}
/>

#### Using `aria-labelledby`

<Canvas
  of={DataTableStories.WithAriaLabelledBy}
  source={{
    code: `
  <div>
    <div id="table-caption">
      A list of <Link target="_blank" href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Crocodile">crocodile</Link> facts
    </div>
    <DataTable>
      <DataTable.Table aria-labelledby="table-caption">
        //...
      </DataTable.Table>
    </DataTable>
  </div>
`,
  }}
/>

### Accessibility test status

{/* Get accessibility tests from the USWDS https://designsystem.digital.gov/components/overview/ -- */}

#### General tests

| Test                                                                                                                                                                       | WCAG Level                                                                                        | Status                                   |
| -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
| **Color is not the only method used to convey meaning in a table.** When viewing the table, you never have to rely on color alone to understand the table’s meaning.       | **A** ([1.4.1 Use of color](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/use-of-color))            | <A11yTestStatus status="conditional"  /> |
| **Table content meets color contrast requirements.** Contrast between the table background and text is at least 4.5:1.                                                     | **AA** ([1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum)](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/contrast-minimum)) | <A11yTestStatus status="success" />      |
| **Table content is visible and functional when screen is magnified.** When you zoom to 200%, content in the table remains visible and does not overlap with other content. | **AA** ([1.4.10 Resize text](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/reflow))                 | <A11yTestStatus status="conditional" />  |

#### Keyboard navigation tests

| Test                                                                                                                                                                                                 | WCAG Level                                                                                     | Status                              |
| ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- |
| **Table elements can be navigated by keyboard without special cues.** When using a keyboard to navigate a table, you can navigate using arrow keys or the tab key with no unexpected instruction.    | **A** ([2.1.1 Keyboard](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/keyboard))                 | <A11yTestStatus status="success" /> |
| **Table cells do not trap focus.** When using a screen reader and keyboard keystrokes to navigate a table, you can easily navigate in and out of the table cells using only keystrokes.              | **A** ([2.1.2 No keyboard trap](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/no-keyboard-trap)) | <A11yTestStatus status="success" /> |
| **Focus indicator is clear on interactive items in tables.** When using a keyboard to navigate a table, a focus indicator appears around all interactive elements (e.g., arrows for sorting, links). | **AA** ([2.4.7 Focus visible](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/focus-visible))      | <A11yTestStatus status="success" /> |

#### Screen reader tests

| Test                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   | WCAG Level                                                                                                 | Status                                           |
| ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------ |
| **Table cells are read one at a time and provide context for each cell.** When using a screen reader to navigate a table, the screen reader speaks one cell at a time and explains where each cell is in the table.                                                    | **A** ([1.3.1 Info and relationships](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/info-and-relationships)) | <A11yTestStatus status="success" isManualTest /> |
| **Table cells are read in logical order.** When using a screen reader to navigate a table, you hear the table cells for an entire row read in the correct order.                                                                                                       | **A** ([1.3.2 Meaningful sequence](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/meaningful-sequence))       | <A11yTestStatus status="success" isManualTest /> |
| **The table has a header, and it is associated with the correct table cells.**                                                                                                                                                                                         | **AA** ([2.4.6 Headings and labels](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/headings-and-labels))      | <A11yTestStatus status="success" isManualTest /> |
| **Table elements have the appropriate name, role, and state.** When using a screen reader to navigate a table, the screen reader announces the name and role of interactive elements. For sortable tables, you hear whether the column is sorted and how it is sorted. | **A** ([4.1.2 Name, role, value](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/name-role-value))             | <A11yTestStatus status="success" isManualTest /> |

## API

### Props

The `DataTable` component has all the props present on a
[HTMLDivElement](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/HTMLDivElement)
and provides the following additional props:

| Prop              | Type                                                                                                                                         | required | default     | Description                                            |
| ----------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------- | ----------- | ------------------------------------------------------ |
| **`appliedSort`** | `AppliedSort`<br /><code>\{<br />  column: string \| number<br />  direction: 'ascending' \| 'descending'<br />\}</code> | No       | `undefined` | Describes how the table data is sorted.                |
| **`onSort`**      | `(sort: AppliedSort) => void`                                                                                                                | No       | `undefined` | Callback invoked when the sort is changed by the user. |

#### DataTable.Table props

The `DataTable.Table` component has all the props present on
a [HTMLTableElement](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/HTMLTableElement).

#### DataTable.Header props

The `DataTable.Header` component is a collection component provided by React Aria.

#### DataTable.Column props

The `DataTable.Column` component is a collection component provided by React Aria.
It does not rely on any native element and offers the following props:

| Prop                | Type                | required | default        | Description                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |
| ------------------- | ------------------- | -------- | -------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **`align`**         | `'left' \| 'right'` | No       | `'left'`       | Sets the text alignment of the column header content.                                                                                                                                                                            |
| **`isRowHeader`**   | `boolean`           | No       | `false`        | Whether a column is a [row header](https://www.w3.org/TR/wai-aria-1.1/#rowheader) and should be announced by assistive technology during row navigation. By default, `DataTable` will assign the first column as the row header. |
| **`width`**         | `string \| number`  | No       | `'1fr'`        | See [Column width](#column-width).                                                                                                                                                                                               |
| **`minWidth`**      | `number`            | No       | `80`           | See [Column width](#column-width).                                                                                                                                                                                               |
| **`maxWidth`**      | `number`            | No       | n/a            | See [Column width](#column-width).                                                                                                                                                                                               |
| **`allowsSorting`** | `boolean`           | No       | `false`        | Whether the column can be sorted by the user. The `onSort` prop on the `DataTable` must be provided for this to work.                                                                                                            |
| **`key`**           | `string \| number`  | No       | auto-generated | Unique identifier for the data represented by the column. It is required when `allowsSorting` is `true` to identify the column to sort by.                                                                                       |

#### DataTable.Cell props

The `DataTable.Cell` component is a collection component provided by React Aria.
It does not rely on any native element and offers the following props:

| Prop          | Type                | required | default  | Description                                                                                                        |
| ------------- | ------------------- | -------- | -------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| **`align`**   | `'left' \| 'right'` | No       | `'left'` | Sets the text alignment of the column header content.                                                              |
| **`colSpan`** | `number`            | No       | n/a      | See [`colspan` MDN documentation](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Reference/Elements/td#colspan) |

#### DataTable.Body props

The `DataTable.Body` component is a collection component provided by React Aria.
It has all the props present on a
[HTMLTableSectionElement](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/HTMLTableSectionElement).

#### DataTable.Row props

The `DataTable.Row` component is a collection component provided by React Aria.
It does not rely on any native element and offers the following props:

| Prop      | Type               | required | default | Description                                            |
| --------- | ------------------ | -------- | ------- | ------------------------------------------------------ |
| **`key`** | `number \| string` | Yes      | n/a     | Unique identifier for the data represented by the row. |

#### DataTable.LoadingState props

The `DataTable.LoadingState` component is a custom collection component that
must be included as an immediate descendent of `DataTable.Body`. It will render
a series of rows and columns with `SkeletonBar` components. It does not rely on
any native element and offers the following props:

| Prop           | Type     | required | default | Description                           |
| -------------- | -------- | -------- | ------- | ------------------------------------- |
| **`rowCount`** | `number` | No       | `3`     | The number of loading rows to render. |

#### DataTable.EmptyState props

The `DataTable.EmptyState` component is a custom collection component that must
be included as an immediate descendent of `DataTable.Body`. It will render an
illustration with a generic message that can be overidden by providing
`children`. It has all the props present on a
[HTMLDivElement](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/HTMLDivElement)
and offers the following props:

| Prop           | Type        | required | default         | Description                              |
| -------------- | ----------- | -------- | --------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
| **`children`** | `ReactNode` | No       | Generic message | Content to override the generic message. |

#### DataTable.ErrorState props

The `DataTable.ErrorState` component is a custom collection component that must
be included as an immediate descendent of `DataTable.Body`. It will render an
illustration with a generic message that can be overidden by providing
`children`. It has all the props present on a
[HTMLDivElement](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/HTMLDivElement)
and offers the following props:

| Prop           | Type        | required | default         | Description                              |
| -------------- | ----------- | -------- | --------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
| **`children`** | `ReactNode` | No       | Generic message | Content to override the generic message. |

## How it works

This section is here purely to give you context on how the `DataTable` component
was built. These implementation details will not impact you directly, but you
may be surprised by what you see if you use the
[React Developer Tools](https://react.dev/learn/react-developer-tools) on a
`DataTable`. The following explanation will help you understand how the
component actually works.

The `DataTable` is a complex component and it is not as flexible as other
Burrito components. It is built on top of React Aria's
[useTable](https://react-spectrum.adobe.com/react-aria/useTable.html#table-header)
and React Stately's
[useTableState](https://react-spectrum.adobe.com/react-stately/useTableState.html).
These hooks require the use of React Stately's components `Cell`, `Column`,
`Row`, `TableBody`, and `TableHeader` which are
[collection components](https://react-spectrum.adobe.com/react-stately/collections.html).

Collection components are a JSX-based API that provide a configuration to their
parent, but are never actually rendered. When used as children, they are
provided to React Stately to be converted into an explicit data structure. This
data structure is then internally used by internal ("inner") React components.

As a consequence of this, the `DataTable` component — specifically the internal
call to `useTableState` — will throw an error if the `children` provided to the
`DataTable.Table` are not the expected collection components. Since these
`children` may be composed dynamically, it is recommended that you wrap the
`DataTable` in a React `ErrorBoundary` component to contain the error. View the
[using an error boundary](#using-an-error-boundary) example above for a code
snippet of how to do this.

Most of the `DataTable` sub-components available to you are collection
components. Actual React components are used under the hood to render DOM
elements based on the data extracted from the collection components.

## Design

<FigmaLink href="https://www.figma.com/design/pDDlKkiRPt9NA2JdA6eyeY/%F0%9F%8C%AF-Burrito-Design-System-UI-Kit?node-id=17531-4122">
  Data Table component in UI Kit
</FigmaLink>

## Usage

### Default Usage

<Canvas
  of={DividerStories.Default}
  sourceState="shown"

const MyApp = () => {

<Canvas of={DividerStories.Sizes} />

## Accessibility

The Divider is a presentational element rendered as an `hr` HTML element. It
does not receive focus and is ignored by assistive technologies.

## API

### Props

The Divider component has all the props present on an
[HTMLElement](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/HTMLElement) as
well as the following:
